steps.prompt: Which word is the first imperative verb?

hints.urbe: 'urbe' is a noun in the ablative case meaning 'the city'
hints.Catilina: 'Catilina' is a proper noun in the vocative case
hints.libera: 'libera' is also a verb (imperative), but 'Egredere' is the first imperative

hints.to enter: 'to enter' is 'ingredi' — the opposite direction; 'egredi' means 'to exit'
hints.to stand: 'to stand' is 'stare' — 'egredi' is a deponent meaning 'to go out'

hints.Present indicative: Present indicative passive/deponent 2nd person would be 'egrederis' — 'Egredere' is imperative
hints.Present infinitive: Infinitive would be 'egredi' — 'Egredere' is the singular imperative of this deponent verb
hints.Future indicative: Future would be 'egrederis' — 'Egredere' is an imperative command

hints.gradior, gradi, gressus sum: These are the parts of 'gradi' (to step/walk) — the base verb without the e- prefix
hints.ingredior, ingredi, ingressus sum: These are the parts of 'ingredi' (to enter) — a different compound of 'gradi'
hints.aggredior, aggredi, aggressus sum: These are the parts of 'aggredi' (to attack) — a different compound of 'gradi'

hints.Indicative: Indicative would state a fact — 'libera' is a command (imperative singular of 'liberare')
hints.Subjunctive: Subjunctive would be 'liberes' — 'libera' is the direct imperative form

hints.liber, libera, liberum: These are adjective forms meaning 'free' — not verb principal parts
hints.delibero, deliberare, deliberavi, delibe: These are the parts of 'deliberare' (to deliberate) — a different verb
hints.labor, labi, lapsus sum: These are the parts of 'labi' (to slip/fall) — entirely different

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'urbs' — 'urbe' with 'ex' is ablative of separation
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'urbem' — 'ex' governs the ablative
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'urbi' — 'urbe' after 'ex' is ablative

hints.1st: 1st declension ablative ends in -a — 'urbs, urbis' is 3rd declension
hints.2nd: 2nd declension ablative ends in -o — 'urbs, urbis' is 3rd declension
hints.5th: 5th declension ablative ends in -e too, but 'urbs, urbis' with gen. -is is 3rd declension

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'metus' — 'metu' is ablative of separation (free from fear)
hints.Accusative: Accusative would be 'metum' — 'metu' is ablative (4th declension abl. sing. = -u)

hints.Nominative: Nominative would be 'res publica' — 'rem publicam' is the accusative object of 'libera'
hints.Dative: Dative would be 'rei publicae' — 'rem publicam' is accusative
hints.Ablative: Ablative would be 're publica' — 'rem publicam' is the accusative direct object

translation: Go forth from the city, Catiline; free the republic from fear.
